data.title: Audiology Services Service Page
data.page_title: Audiology Services | America's Best Hearing
data.meta_description: Audiology Services at America's Best Hearing.

children.text: America’s Best Hearing provides expert hearing care to help you stay connected to the sounds that matter most. From comprehensive hearing evaluations to advanced hearing aid solutions, our audiologists and hearing aid specialists in Michigan, Minnesota, and Florida are here to support your hearing health with personalized care.

content_blocks.body_content: At America’s Best Hearing, your journey to better hearing begins with a complete evaluation. Our audiologists and hearing instrument specialists use advanced testing to determine how well you hear in different environments and to uncover any underlying issues. This thorough process allows us to create customized recommendations designed to fit your needs, lifestyle, and goals.

content_blocks.accordion_title: Which hearing test is the best?
content_blocks.accordion_body:
The “best” hearing test depends on your situation. A full evaluation with an audiologist is the most accurate way to measure hearing ability. Common tests include:

  • Pure-tone audiometry
  • Speech audiometry
  • Tympanometry
  • Otoacoustic emissions (OAE)
  • Auditory brainstem response (ABR)

These tests together provide the most reliable diagnosis and help guide the right treatment.

content_blocks.accordion_title: Can I test my own hearing?
content_blocks.accordion_body: You may notice warning signs of hearing loss on your own—turning up the TV, asking people to repeat themselves, or struggling in noisy settings. Apps and online tools can give you a rough idea, but they aren’t a substitute for professional testing. Only a licensed audiologist can provide a precise diagnosis and ensure that any hearing difficulty isn’t linked to a medical issue.

content_blocks.accordion_title: Are online hearing tests any good?
content_blocks.accordion_body: Online tests can be a helpful first step, but they are limited. They typically measure whether you can hear certain tones, but they cannot evaluate speech clarity, diagnose the type of loss, or detect medical conditions. Results can also vary depending on your device or background noise. If you’re concerned about your hearing, schedule an in-person evaluation for an accurate, personalized assessment.

content_blocks.body_content: At America’s Best Hearing, we go beyond basic hearing checks. Our diagnostic services provide a deeper look into your hearing health, balance, and ear function. These tests are important for identifying not just hearing loss, but also conditions like tinnitus, middle ear issues, or balance disorders. Accurate diagnosis ensures we recommend the right path forward – whether that’s hearing aids, medical care, or other treatment options.

content_blocks.accordion_title: What types of diagnostic tests are performed during a hearing evaluation?
content_blocks.accordion_body:
A diagnostic hearing evaluation may include:

  • Pure-tone and speech audiometry
  • Tympanometry for middle ear health
  • Otoacoustic emissions (OAE) for inner ear function
  • Auditory brainstem response (ABR) testing for neurological evaluation

content_blocks.accordion_title: When should I get a diagnostic hearing test?
content_blocks.accordion_body:
Consider scheduling a test if you notice:

  • Trouble hearing conversations in quiet or noisy places
  • Ringing or buzzing in your ears (tinnitus)
  • Muffled or distorted sounds
  • A sensation of fullness in your ears
  • Sudden or gradual changes in hearing
  • Dizziness or balance concerns

Prompt testing helps us detect and address issues early so you can stay connected and protected.

content_blocks.body_content: From choosing the right device to long-term care, America’s Best Hearing is with you every step of the way. Our audiologists and hearing instrument specialists provide expert fittings, adjustments, cleanings, and repairs to ensure your hearing aids deliver the clearest sound possible.

A precise fitting makes all the difference. We program each device to your unique hearing needs, test the fit for comfort, and use real-ear measurement technology to confirm that sound levels are exactly where they should be. Follow-up visits are included to fine-tune performance as you adjust.

content_blocks.accordion_title: How long does it take to get fitted for a hearing aid?
content_blocks.accordion_body: Most fittings take one to two hours. If a custom earmold is needed, the process may extend by a week or two. After your initial fitting, we schedule check-ins to make sure your hearing aids continue to meet your needs.

content_blocks.accordion_title: Can you get a hearing aid without a test?
content_blocks.accordion_body: Over-the-counter hearing aids exist, but they are only suitable for adults with mild to moderate loss. A professional hearing test ensures your device is customized for your hearing profile, rules out medical concerns, and provides the best chance for long-term success.

content_blocks.body_content: Even high-quality hearing aids need maintenance. At America’s Best Hearing, we provide fast and effective repair services, from quick in-office cleanings to more complex manufacturer repairs.

content_blocks.accordion_title: What are common problems that require hearing aid repair?
content_blocks.accordion_body:
• Weak or no sound
  • Distorted or muffled audio
  • Whistling or feedback
  • Physical damage or broken parts

content_blocks.accordion_title: How long does it take to repair a hearing aid?
content_blocks.accordion_body: Minor issues can often be fixed the same day in our office. More advanced repairs may require sending your device to the manufacturer and can take a few days to a few weeks.

content_blocks.accordion_title: Can I fix my hearing aid at home?
content_blocks.accordion_body: Some problems (like replacing batteries, cleaning filters, or drying out moisture) can be handled at home. But for anything more complex, it’s best to see a professional to avoid further damage.

content_blocks.body_content:
Protecting your hearing is just as important as restoring it. At America’s Best Hearing, we offer custom-fit solutions for every lifestyle:

  • For Musicians – Custom earplugs and in-ear monitors that lower volume without distorting music.
  • For Hunters – Electronic earplugs that block harmful gunfire while letting you hear your surroundings.
  • For Workers – Industrial-strength protection that safeguards your ears in loud environments.
  • For Everyday Use – Comfortable, custom plugs for swimming, travel, or general noise protection.

content_blocks.accordion_title: Why should I use hearing protection?
content_blocks.accordion_body: Because once your hearing is gone, it can’t be restored. Custom protection reduces the risk of permanent damage without sacrificing comfort or clarity.

content_blocks.accordion_title: Are custom earplugs better than store-bought ones?
content_blocks.accordion_body: Yes. Custom plugs are molded to your ear, so they fit better, feel more comfortable, and block harmful sounds more effectively than generic options.

content_blocks.accordion_title: Can I still hear people talking with earplugs in?
content_blocks.accordion_body: Absolutely. Many custom options are designed to filter harmful noise while still letting you hear speech clearly, making them ideal for both work and social environments.

data.title: Ear Wax Removal Service Page
data.page_title: Ear Wax Removal | America's Best Hearing
data.meta_description: Ear Wax Removal at America's Best Hearing.

children.text: America’s Best Hearing provides professional ear wax removal in Michigan, Minnesota, and Florida.

Ear wax is natural and important. It helps protect your ears by catching dirt, dust, and bacteria before they reach the eardrum. Most of the time, your ears clean themselves. But sometimes, too much ear wax builds up and causes blockages. When this happens, you may notice muffled hearing, pressure, or even pain.

At America’s Best Hearing, our audiologists and hearing instrument specialists provide safe and gentle ear wax removal for patients across Michigan, Minnesota, and Florida, specifically in these locations:
Lansing, MIPortage, MIMaple Grove, MNEden Prairie, MNMendota Heights, MNNew Ulm, MNWillmar, MNRoseville, MNLake Wales, FLViera, FLSebring, FL
With professional care, we can help you hear more clearly and feel more comfortable.

content_blocks.body_content:
Ear wax, also called cerumen, is made by small glands inside your ear canal. It keeps your ears healthy by:

  • Trapping dirt and dust
  • Preventing bacteria from growing
  • Protecting the ear canal from dryness

Normally, ear wax moves out on its own as you chew or move your jaw. But sometimes, it doesn’t clear properly and builds up. This is when professional help may be needed. America’s Best Hearing provides professional ear wax removal in Michigan, Minnesota, and Florida.

content_blocks.heading_text: Signs of Too Much Ear Wax

content_blocks.body_content:
You may have a wax blockage if you notice:

  • Muffled or blocked hearing
  • Ear pain or a feeling of pressure
  • Ringing or buzzing in your ears (tinnitus)
  • Itching or irritation inside the ear
  • Dizziness or balance problems
  • Coughing caused by ear pressure

If these symptoms sound familiar, a professional ear wax removal appointment can bring quick relief.

content_blocks.heading_text: Why You Shouldn’t Remove Ear Wax at Home

content_blocks.body_content:
It’s common to reach for cotton swabs, ear candles, or even hairpins to clean your ears. But these methods often push the wax deeper and can cause damage. Some at-home attempts may even injure the eardrum. Safer ways to manage ear wax at home include:

  • Using over-the-counter drops to soften wax
  • Wiping the outer ear gently with a soft cloth
  • Allowing your ears to self-clean naturally

If symptoms continue, it’s best to visit an audiologist or hearing specialist for safe, professional care.

content_blocks.accordion_title: How Often Should You Have Ear Wax Removed?
content_blocks.accordion_body:
Most people don’t need regular ear wax removal. But some may need professional cleaning more often if they:
  • Wear hearing aids or earbuds every day
  • Have small or curved ear canals
  • Suffer from frequent ear infections
  • Have skin conditions like eczema or psoriasis
Our audiologist or hearing instrument specialist can recommend a schedule that works best for you.

content_blocks.accordion_title: Can ear wax affect hearing aids?
content_blocks.accordion_body: Yes. Ear wax is one of the most common reasons hearing aids stop working. Wax can clog the small openings in a device, making sounds weaker or cutting them out completely. Regular ear care helps your hearing aids last longer and work better.

content_blocks.accordion_title: Is ear wax normal?
content_blocks.accordion_body: Yes! Everyone makes ear wax, and it’s a healthy part of your ears. It only becomes a problem when too much builds up.

content_blocks.accordion_title: Does ear wax removal hurt?
content_blocks.accordion_body: No. Professional removal is safe and usually painless. You may feel a little pressure, but most people find the process quick and comfortable.

content_blocks.accordion_title: How long does ear wax removal take?
content_blocks.accordion_body: Most appointments last about 15 to 30 minutes, depending on how much wax is present.

content_blocks.accordion_title: Can children get ear wax removed?
content_blocks.accordion_body: Yes. Kids can also develop ear wax blockages. Our audiologists and hearing specialists are trained to work gently with children to keep them comfortable.

content_blocks.accordion_title: Will removing ear wax improve my hearing?
content_blocks.accordion_body: If your ears are blocked by wax, professional removal often makes a big difference right away. Sounds may seem clearer, and you may notice immediate relief.

content_blocks.accordion_title: Can I prevent ear wax buildup?
content_blocks.accordion_body: You can’t always prevent it, but avoiding cotton swabs and keeping up with regular check-ups can help. If you wear hearing aids, regular ear cleanings are especially important.

content_blocks.body_content:
At America’s Best Hearing, your hearing health is our priority. We provide:

  • Safe, gentle removal methods tailored to your needs
  • Care from licensed audiologists and hearing instrument specialists
  • Comfortable, stress-free appointments
  • Support for both ear health and hearing aid performance

content_blocks.heading_text: Ear Wax Removal in Michigan, Minnesota & Florida

content_blocks.body_content:
Don’t let ear wax block your hearing or cause discomfort. The team at America’s Best Hearing is here to help you hear clearly and feel better.

Schedule your professional ear wax removal appointment today at our locations in Michigan, Minnesota, and Florida.

data.title: Hearing Aid Fittings Service Page
data.page_title: Hearing Aid Fittings | America's Best Hearing
data.meta_description: Hearing Aid Fittings at America's Best Hearing.

children.text: America’s Best Hearing provides expert hearing aid fittings in Michigan, Minnesota, and Florida.

A hearing aid fitting is more than just placing devices in your ears. It’s a personalized process where our audiologists and hearing instrument specialists carefully adjust your hearing aids to match your unique hearing needs.
Professional hearing aid fittings ensure that speech sounds crisp, background noise is managed, and everyday listening feels as natural and comfortable as possible.

content_blocks.heading_text: Why is a Professional Hearing Aid Fitting Important?

content_blocks.body_content: Hearing aids do far more than make sounds louder. They need to be programmed to your specific hearing loss for the best results. A professional fitting helps you hear speech more clearly in both quiet and noisy environments. You won’t also have to deal with discomfort from sounds that are too loud or distorted With the right fitting, your hearing aids become an effective tool to reconnect you with the sounds you’ve been missing.

content_blocks.heading_text: Why Should an Audiologist or Hearing Specialist Fit Your Hearing Aids?

content_blocks.body_content:
Audiologists and hearing instrument specialists are trained to understand how hearing loss affects your daily life. They have the expertise to:

  • Adjust hearing aids using advanced software and real-ear measurements
  • Ensure the devices fit comfortably and securely
  • Teach you how to use and care for your hearing aids properly

A professional fitting prevents issues like discomfort, poor sound quality, or frustration that can come from improperly adjusted devices. Reach out to America’s Best Hearing to schedule an appointment with audiologists and hearing instrument specialists in Florida, Michigan, and Minnesota.

content_blocks.accordion_title: What Happens During a Hearing Aid Fitting?
content_blocks.accordion_body:
When you come to America’s Best Hearing, your fitting may include:

  1. Reviewing your hearing test results – Understanding your type and level of hearing loss.
  2. Choosing the right hearing aid – Selecting a device that fits your lifestyle, preferences, and budget.
  3. Programming the hearing aids – Using software to adjust settings to your hearing profile.
  4. Real-ear measurement (REM) – Testing sound levels inside your ear canal to ensure accuracy.
  5. Hands-on training – Learning how to insert, remove, and care for your hearing aids.
  6. Follow-up planning – Scheduling additional visits for adjustments as you get used to your devices.

content_blocks.accordion_title: What to Expect
content_blocks.accordion_body: Your first fitting is an exciting step toward better hearing. At first, some sounds may seem new or louder than expected. Over time, your brain will adjust, and these sounds will blend naturally into your daily life. Our team at America’s Best Hearing will be there to guide you, answer questions, and make sure your devices are working as they should. Contact us to be connected to expert audiologists and hearing instrument specialists in Michigan, Minnesota, and Florida.

content_blocks.accordion_title: Adjusting to Hearing Aids
content_blocks.accordion_body: Most people need a few weeks to fully adjust. For starters, we encourage you to wear your hearing aids consistently. You can also practice listening in quiet environments before tackling noisy places. Be patient with yourself. Remember, your brain is learning to hear again.

content_blocks.accordion_title: Follow-Up Appointments
content_blocks.accordion_body: Follow-up visits are a vital part of the fitting process at America’s Best Hearing. If your hearing aids sound too loud, too soft, or uncomfortable, we can fine-tune them. These adjustments ensure your devices continue to perform at their best as you adapt to them.

content_blocks.accordion_title: Are hearing aid fittings covered by insurance?
content_blocks.accordion_body: Coverage varies by provider. Traditional Medicare generally does not cover hearing aids, but some Medicare Advantage plans, private insurance, and Veterans Affairs benefits may help. Our team can assist you in reviewing your coverage.

content_blocks.accordion_title: How can I make my hearing aid fit better?
content_blocks.accordion_body: If your device feels uncomfortable, your audiologist can adjust it. Custom earmolds or different ear tips may also improve the fit. Never force a hearing aid into your ear—it should feel secure but comfortable.

content_blocks.accordion_title: How long does a hearing aid fitting take?
content_blocks.accordion_body: The first fitting usually takes about an hour. It may take a few follow-up visits over several weeks to fine-tune your devices for the best performance.

content_blocks.heading_text: Hearing Aid Fittings in Michigan, Minnesota & Florida

content_blocks.body_content:
At America’s Best Hearing, our audiologists and hearing instrument specialists are committed to helping you get the most out of your hearing aids. We take the time to ensure your devices are comfortable, properly adjusted, and delivering the clearest sound possible.

If you or a loved one needs a hearing aid fitting, schedule an appointment today at one of our clinics in Michigan, Minnesota, or Florida.

content_blocks.accordion_title: Hearing Assessments

content_blocks.accordion_title: Hearing Aid Styles

content_blocks.accordion_title: Hearing Aid Fittings

content_blocks.accordion_title: Programming and Adjustments

content_blocks.accordion_title: Counseling and Education
content_blocks.accordion_body:
Learning to use hearing aids takes time, and we’re here to guide you. We provide:

content_blocks.accordion_title: Real Ear Measurements
content_blocks.accordion_body: We use real ear measurement (REM) testing to confirm that your hearing aids are programmed correctly for your ears. A small microphone is placed in your ear to measure sound levels in real time, ensuring your devices deliver the most accurate amplification possible.

content_blocks.accordion_title: Hearing Aid Repairs & Maintenance

data.title: Hearing Tests Service Page
data.page_title: Hearing Tests | America's Best Hearing
data.meta_description: Hearing Tests at America's Best Hearing.

children.text: America’s Best Hearing provides comprehensive hearing tests in Michigan, Minnesota, and Florida.

A hearing test is the first step toward better hearing health. At America’s Best Hearing, our audiologists and hearing instrument specialists use advanced, professional testing to identify hearing problems and recommend solutions that fit your life.
With locations across Michigan, Minnesota, and Florida, our team is here to support your hearing health every step of the way.

content_blocks.heading_text: Why Would I Need a Hearing Test?

content_blocks.body_content:
Hearing loss often develops gradually, making it easy to miss the early signs. Some common red flags include:

  • Difficulty following conversations, especially in noisy places
  • Asking people to repeat themselves often
  • Turning up the volume on the TV or phone
  • Hearing ringing, buzzing, or humming sounds (tinnitus)

If you notice these signs, it may be time to schedule a professional hearing evaluation.

content_blocks.heading_text: What Are the Types of Hearing Tests?

content_blocks.body_content:
Our comprehensive evaluations may include:

  • Pure-Tone Audiometry – Identifies the quietest sounds you can detect at various frequencies.
  • Speech Audiometry – Determines how well you recognize and understand speech at different volumes.
  • Tympanometry – Detects middle ear issues such as fluid buildup, infections, or eardrum problems.

Contact us today to learn more. We provide hearing tests in Florida, Michigan, and Minnesota.

content_blocks.heading_text: Three Main Types of Hearing Loss

content_blocks.body_content:
• Sensorineural Hearing Loss – The most common type, caused by damage to the inner ear or auditory nerve. It is usually permanent and managed with hearing aids or, in some cases, cochlear implants.
  • Conductive Hearing Loss – Occurs when sound cannot pass through the outer or middle ear. Often treatable with medication or surgery.
  • Mixed Hearing Loss – A combination of sensorineural and conductive hearing loss, requiring customized treatment.

content_blocks.accordion_title: Hearing Test: What to Expect
content_blocks.accordion_body: A hearing test is safe, simple, and painless. During your appointment, you’ll wear headphones and listen to a series of sounds at different pitches and volumes. Your provider will also review your hearing history and check the health of your ears. Most tests take 30 to 45 minutes to complete.

content_blocks.accordion_title: Hearing Test Results
content_blocks.accordion_body: After testing, our team will explain your results using an audiogram, a visual chart of your hearing ability. If hearing loss is detected, we’ll discuss the best treatment options for you, whether that’s hearing aids, protective strategies, or a referral for medical care.

content_blocks.accordion_title: Can I test my own hearing?
content_blocks.accordion_body: You can try online tests or apps, but they are not as accurate as a professional evaluation. At America’s Best Hearing, we provide complete in-person hearing tests across Michigan, Minnesota, and Florida.

content_blocks.accordion_title: Will I need to do anything to prepare?
content_blocks.accordion_body: No special preparation is required, but we recommend avoiding loud noise before your test.

content_blocks.accordion_title: Are hearing tests safe?
content_blocks.accordion_body: Yes, hearing tests are completely safe and painless.

content_blocks.accordion_title: How reliable are online hearing tests?
content_blocks.accordion_body: Online tests may give you a general sense of your hearing ability but cannot provide a full diagnosis. A professional evaluation is the most accurate way to understand your hearing health.

content_blocks.accordion_title: Can I take a hearing test on my phone?
content_blocks.accordion_body: Some apps offer basic screenings, but they cannot replace the precision of a clinical evaluation with an audiologist.

content_blocks.accordion_title: How often should I get a hearing test?
content_blocks.accordion_body:
• Adults with normal hearing: every 3–5 years
  • Adults with hearing loss or hearing aids: once a year
  • Adults exposed to loud environments: once a year

content_blocks.heading_text: Hearing Tests in Michigan, Minnesota & Florida

content_blocks.body_content:
Your hearing is vital to your quality of life. Our audiologists and hearing instrument specialists at America’s Best Hearing are dedicated to helping you stay connected to the sounds that matter most.

Schedule your hearing test today and take the first step toward better hearing.

data.page_title: Custom Hearing Protection | America's Best Hearing
data.meta_description: Custom Hearing Protection at America's Best Hearing.

children.text: America’s Best Hearing provides custom hearing protection in Michigan, Minnesota, and Florida.

Protecting your hearing is essential, whether you’re working in a loud environment, enjoying music, hunting, swimming, or simply trying to get a good night’s sleep. At America’s Best Hearing, our audiologists and hearing instrument specialists in Michigan, Minnesota, and Florida provide custom hearing protection designed for long-term ear health, comfort, and safety.
From musicians and hunters to industrial workers and light sleepers, we offer ready-made and custom-fit options. These include earplugs, earmuffs, and advanced digital hearing protection.
Our team at America’s Best Hearing will help you find the best solution for your lifestyle.

content_blocks.accordion_title: Ready-Made Hearing Protection
content_blocks.accordion_body:
• Pre-molded options are affordable and convenient, though not as tailored as custom devices.

    • Earplugs – Small, portable protection worn in the ear canal.
      • Pros: Inexpensive, easy to carry.
      • Cons: May feel less secure or comfortable.
    • Semi-Insert Earplugs – Connected by a band and rest at the canal entrance.
      • Pros: Reusable and easy to wear.
      • Cons: Lower noise reduction.
    • Earmuffs – Cover the entire ear to block external sounds.
      • Pros: Strong protection.
      • Cons: Bulkier, may feel warm or uncomfortable with long use.

content_blocks.accordion_title: Custom Hearing Protection
content_blocks.accordion_body:
As their name suggests, custom hearing protection is made from ear impressions to provide the best fit and performance.

  • Custom Earplugs – Great for musicians, workers, or everyday noise protection.
    • Pros: Comfortable and long-lasting.
    • Cons: Requires custom fitting. Contact America’s Best Hearing to learn more about custom earplugs in Michigan, Minnesota, and Florida.
  • Impulse Filter Earplugs – Designed for hunters. Block loud gunshots while allowing softer sounds.
    • Pros: Smart, responsive protection.
    • Cons: Best for short bursts of sound, not constant noise.
  • Sleep Plugs – Soft plugs made for side sleepers.
    • Pros: Comfortable and snugly fit for overnight use.
    • Cons: May block important alerts like alarms.
  • In-Ear Monitors – For musicians and performers who need sound clarity on stage.
    • Pros: Exceptional sound quality.
    • Cons: Higher cost than standard plugs.
  • SoundGear Digital Protection – Advanced digital ear protection for shooting or high-noise environments.
    • Pros: Reduces harmful sounds while keeping speech clear.
    • Cons: Premium price.

content_blocks.body_content:
At America’s Best Hearing, we tailor solutions to your lifestyle:

  • Hunting – Impulse filter plugs and SoundGear protect against gunfire while preserving awareness.
  • Music – Musician’s plugs and in-ear monitors reduce volume without distorting sound.
  • Swimming – Swim plugs prevent water from entering and reduce the risk of swimmer’s ear.
  • Sleeping – Sleep plugs block snoring and outside noise for better rest.
  • Work & Industry – Heavy-duty plugs and earmuffs for construction, factory, or shop work.
  • Water Sports – Surf plugs protect against wind, water, and cold to prevent surfer’s ear.

We offer ear molds in different materials for the perfect fit:

  • Vinyl – Soft, flexible, and hypoallergenic. Best for children or sensitive ears.
  • Silicone – Durable, water-resistant, and flexible. Good for active users.
  • Acrylic – Hard, lightweight, and easy to clean. Durable, but less ideal for sensitive ears.

Custom molds can be made for hearing aids, swimming, sleeping, or noise protection.

content_blocks.accordion_title: How Custom Ear Molds Are Made
content_blocks.accordion_body: Our specialists take a quick, painless impression of your ear canal. That impression is sent to a lab, where your custom ear mold is crafted from your chosen material. The result is a snug, secure fit that provides better comfort and sound protection than standard options.

content_blocks.accordion_title: How Long Do Custom Earplugs Last?
content_blocks.accordion_body: With proper care, custom earplugs typically last 3 to 5 years. Their lifespan depends on the material and frequency of use. Regular cleaning and occasional fit checks (especially for children or people with ear changes) help maintain effectiveness.

content_blocks.heading_text: Custom Hearing Protection in Michigan, Minnesota & Florida

content_blocks.body_content:
At America’s Best Hearing, we’re committed to helping you protect your hearing for the future.

Whether you need hearing protection for work, music, hunting, swimming, or sleeping, our experienced audiologists and hearing instrument specialists will provide personalized solutions that keep your ears safe and comfortable.

Schedule your custom fitting today at one of our locations in Michigan, Minnesota, or Florida, and take the first step toward lasting hearing health.

children.text: America’s Best Hearing provides quality hearing aids and products in Michigan, Minnesota, and Florida.

At America’s Best Hearing, we are proud to offer today’s most trusted hearing aids and hearing products. Whether you are just starting to explore your options or already use hearing aids, our team is here to help. We provide hearing tests, custom fittings, adjustments, cleanings, and repairs so your hearing devices always work their best.
We work with leading brands to make sure you get reliable technology that fits your needs, lifestyle, and budget.

content_blocks.heading_text: Hearing Aid Brands We Work With

content_blocks.accordion_title: Phonak Hearing Aids
content_blocks.accordion_body: Phonak has more than 75 years of experience making hearing aids. Popular models like Phonak Lumity improve speech understanding in noise, while Phonak Naída is designed for people with more severe hearing loss. Most Phonak hearing aids offer Bluetooth streaming, rechargeable batteries, and water-resistant designs.

content_blocks.accordion_title: Oticon Hearing Aids
content_blocks.accordion_body: Oticon has established its roots in hearing care since 1904. Currently, their BrainHearing™ technology works with the way your brain processes sound, making speech clearer and reducing listening effort. Models like Oticon Real and Oticon More also offer tinnitus support and wireless connectivity.

content_blocks.accordion_title: Widex Hearing Aids
content_blocks.accordion_body: Widex is known for its clear, natural sound. Models like Widex Moment and Widex SmartRIC reduce delays in sound and make conversations feel lifelike. Widex also offers excellent options for people with tinnitus, with sound therapy programs and rechargeable models.

content_blocks.accordion_title: ReSound Hearing Aids
content_blocks.accordion_body: ReSound has more than 75 years of innovation. Their hearing aids, including ReSound Omnia and ReSound ONE, are designed for natural sound quality and strong Bluetooth performance. Many ReSound devices also provide personalized sound direction and tinnitus support.

content_blocks.accordion_title: Starkey Hearing Aids
content_blocks.accordion_body: Starkey is a U.S.-based company that combines hearing technology with health features. Starkey Genesis AI and Evolv AI offer fall detection, activity tracking, tinnitus support, and hands-free calling. Starkey also offers rechargeable batteries and smartphone connections make them easy to use.

content_blocks.accordion_title: Signia Hearing Aids
content_blocks.accordion_body: Signia hearing aids are sleek, modern, and packed with features. Devices like Signia Pure Charge&Go and Silk X include Bluetooth streaming, rechargeable batteries, and excellent speech clarity in noise. Many models are also nearly invisible and include own-voice processing for a more natural sound.

content_blocks.accordion_title: Unitron Hearing Aids
content_blocks.accordion_body: Unitron focuses on comfort and flexibility. Models like Unitron Blu automatically adjust to different listening environments and include Bluetooth streaming and rechargeable options. Their simple smartphone app makes it easy to fine-tune your hearing aids.

content_blocks.body_content: Hearing protection is just as important as hearing correction. We provide custom earplugs and molds for people who work in loud environments, musicians, hunters, swimmers, and anyone who needs extra protection. Custom products fit better than store-bought versions and block harmful noise while keeping important sounds clear.

content_blocks.accordion_title: Custom Ear Molds
content_blocks.accordion_body: Custom ear molds are shaped to your ear for the best comfort and performance. They can be used with hearing aids, in-ear monitors, swim plugs, and hearing protection. Custom molds improve sound quality, reduce feedback, and help keep devices in place.

content_blocks.accordion_title: Custom Ear Molds for Musicians
content_blocks.accordion_body: Musicians need to protect their hearing while still hearing music clearly. We offer musician earplugs and in-ear monitors designed to lower harmful volume levels without distorting sound quality.

content_blocks.body_content:
Assistive Listening Devices (ALDs) make it easier to hear in certain situations, even if you already use hearing aids. They are helpful for listening to the TV, talking on the phone, or following conversations in groups. Common types include:

  • TV Streamers – Send sound directly from your TV to your hearing aids.
  • FM Systems – Transmit a speaker’s voice straight to your ears in classrooms or meetings.
  • Personal Amplifiers – Portable devices that boost nearby sounds.
  • Hearing Loop Systems – Pick up sound signals in theaters, churches, and public places.
  • Bluetooth Microphones – Help you hear one person clearly in noisy settings.
  • CaptionCall Phones – Provide free real-time captions on a screen while you talk on the phone (requires certification from a hearing professional).
  • CapTel Phones – Similar to CaptionCall, CapTel phones display captions of conversations so you can read what’s being said while you listen.

ALDs help reduce listening effort and keep you connected at home, at work, and in public spaces.

content_blocks.accordion_title: What is the most advanced hearing aid?
content_blocks.accordion_body: The most advanced hearing aids include speech clarity features, Bluetooth streaming, rechargeable batteries, and strong noise reduction. The best one depends on your hearing test and daily needs.

content_blocks.accordion_title: Which hearing aids are best for tinnitus?
content_blocks.accordion_body: Brands like Widex, Oticon, and Starkey include tinnitus sound therapy features. Our team can help match you with the best option.

content_blocks.accordion_title: Which hearing aids are best for severe hearing loss?
content_blocks.accordion_body: Phonak, Oticon, and Starkey all make powerful hearing aids for people with severe to profound hearing loss. Your audiologist will help find the right solution for you.

content_blocks.accordion_title: How long do custom ear molds last?
content_blocks.accordion_body: Custom ear molds usually last 2 to 5 years with proper care.

content_blocks.accordion_title: Are ALDs required in public places?
content_blocks.accordion_body: Yes. Many theaters, churches, schools, and government buildings are required to provide ALDs to help people with hearing loss access sound more easily.

data.title: Hearing Aid Batteries Service Page
data.page_title: Hearing Aid Batteries | America's Best Hearing
data.meta_description: Hearing Aid Batteries at America's Best Hearing.

children.text: America’s Best Hearing provides hearing aid batteries in Michigan, Minnesota, and Florida to ensure that your hearing devices are always functioning at their best.

Hearing aids are powered by small batteries that keep them working day after day. Having the right battery is important to make sure your hearing aids work their best. At America’s Best Hearing, our audiologists and hearing instrument specialists help you find the right size and type of battery for your devices.
With locations in Michigan, Minnesota, and Florida, we make it easy for you to get the supplies you need.

content_blocks.body_content:
Most hearing aids use small, disposable batteries that come in four main sizes. Each size is marked with a color to make it easier to choose:

  • Size 10 (Yellow) – Very small, used for tiny hearing aids like CIC (completely-in-canal).
  • Size 312 (Brown) – Common size, often used in ITC (in-the-canal) and RIC (receiver-in-canal) hearing aids.
  • Size 13 (Orange) – Larger batteries, usually for behind-the-ear hearing aids.
  • Size 675 (Blue) – The largest size, used in high-power hearing aids for severe hearing loss.

Our team can help you figure out which size works with your hearing aids.

content_blocks.heading_text: How Long Do Hearing Aid Batteries Last?

content_blocks.body_content:
Battery life depends on the size of the battery, your type of hearing aid, and how often you use it. On average:

  • Size 10: about 3-5 days
  • Size 312: about 5-7 days
  • Size 13: about 7-10 days
  • Size 675: about 10-14 days

If you stream music or phone calls through your hearing aids, the battery may run out more quickly.

content_blocks.heading_text: Tips for Making Batteries Last Longer

content_blocks.body_content:
• Store in a cool, dry place – Avoid heat and moisture.
  • Keep the tab on until use – Pulling the tab activates the battery.
  • Open the battery door at night – This saves power and lets air dry out the aid.
  • Carry extras – Always keep spare batteries with you in case one runs out.

Many new hearing aids now come with rechargeable batteries. Instead of replacing batteries every few days, you simply place your hearing aids in a charging case overnight. Rechargeable options are more eco-friendly and convenient, but they may not be the right choice for everyone. Our audiologists and hearing instrument specialists in Michigan, Minnesota, and Florida will help you decide if rechargeable hearing aids are a good fit for your lifestyle.

content_blocks.accordion_title: How do I know which battery my hearing aid uses?
content_blocks.accordion_body: The battery size is listed in your hearing aid manual, or our specialists can help you find the right one.

content_blocks.accordion_title: What should I do with used batteries?
content_blocks.accordion_body: Don’t throw them in the trash. Collect them in a safe container and recycle them at a local center or through your hearing clinic.

content_blocks.accordion_title: Can cold weather affect batteries?
content_blocks.accordion_body: Yes. Cold temperatures can make batteries drain faster. Keep spares at room temperature for best results.

content_blocks.accordion_title: Are rechargeable hearing aids better?
content_blocks.accordion_body: Rechargeable hearing aids are convenient and cost less over time, but disposable batteries may still be a better option for some people. Your provider will help you decide what works best for you.

content_blocks.heading_text: Hearing Aid Batteries in Michigan, Minnesota & Florida

content_blocks.body_content:
At America’s Best Hearing, we know how important it is to keep your hearing aids powered and working properly. Whether you use disposable batteries or rechargeable devices, our audiologists and hearing instrument specialists are here to guide you, answer your questions, and make sure you always have what you need.

Visit one of our locations in Michigan, Minnesota, or Florida to stock up on hearing aid batteries or learn more about rechargeable hearing aids today.

data.title: Assistive Listening Devices Service Page
data.page_title: Assistive Listening Devices | America's Best Hearing
data.meta_description: Assistive Listening Devices at America's Best Hearing.

children.text: Even the best hearing aids sometimes need extra help. Busy restaurants, large group events, or even watching TV across the room can still be tough. That’s where assistive listening devices (ALDs) make a difference.

ALDs give your hearing an extra boost in certain situations. Whether you’re watching your favorite show, talking on the phone, or attending a church service, these devices make sounds clearer so you don’t miss the moments that matter most.
At America’s Best Hearing, we see to it that you get the most out of your hearing aids. We offer hearing aid accessories and assistive listening devices in Michigan, Minnesota, and Florida.

content_blocks.heading_text: Hearing Aids vs. Assistive Listening Devices

content_blocks.body_content:
Hearing aids are designed to help with many daily listening needs. But in noisy or crowded places (like classrooms, theaters, or meetings), you may still struggle to hear clearly.
Assistive listening devices focus on specific situations. They can be used on their own for people with mild hearing loss or together with hearing aids for even better results. Many are affordable, easy to use, and don’t require a hearing test.

content_blocks.accordion_title: Hearing Loop Systems
content_blocks.accordion_body:
• Sends sound directly to hearing aids or cochlear implants with a telecoil (T-coil).
  • Benefits: Clear sound with less background noise. Great for public places like theaters, churches, and auditoriums.

content_blocks.accordion_title: Infrared Systems
content_blocks.accordion_body:
• Uses invisible light waves to send sound to a personal receiver.
  • Benefits: Perfect for private listening at home or in courtrooms. Won’t pick up interference from other devices.

content_blocks.accordion_title: FM Systems
content_blocks.accordion_body:
• Uses radio waves to send sound from a speaker’s microphone to the listener’s receiver.
  • Benefits: Great for classrooms, meetings, and lectures. Cuts through background noise and distance.

content_blocks.accordion_title: Personal Amplifiers
content_blocks.accordion_body:
• Small, handheld devices with headphones or earbuds that amplify nearby sounds.
  • Benefits: Simple, budget-friendly, and great for one-on-one talks or TV listening.

content_blocks.accordion_title: TV Listening Systems
content_blocks.accordion_body:
• Send TV sound directly to wireless headphones or compatible receivers.
  • Benefits: Crisp, clear dialogue without turning up the volume. Options include Bluetooth, infrared, and RF.

content_blocks.accordion_title: Alerting Devices
content_blocks.accordion_body:
• Use lights, vibrations, or loud sounds to alert you to alarms, doorbells, or phone calls.
  • Benefits: Improves safety and awareness at home. Helpful for people with more severe hearing loss.

content_blocks.accordion_title: CaptionCall Phones
content_blocks.accordion_body:
• A free phone service (with certification from a hearing professional) that shows real-time captions of your phone conversations on a large screen.
  • Benefits: Easier phone calls, less guessing, and more confidence staying connected. Talk to our audiologist or hearing instrument specialist in Florida, Michigan, or Minnesota to learn more.

content_blocks.accordion_title: CapTel Phones
content_blocks.accordion_body:
• Similar to CaptionCall, CapTel phones display captions of conversations so you can read and listen at the same time.
  • Benefits: Clearer communication over the phone, ideal for those who miss words during calls. Talk to our audiologist or hearing instrument specialist in Michigan, Florida, or Minnesota to learn more.

content_blocks.accordion_title: What is the most common assistive listening device?
content_blocks.accordion_body: FM systems are very popular, especially in classrooms. The speaker wears a microphone, and the listener hears clearly through a receiver even in noisy rooms.

content_blocks.accordion_title: Why are ALDs important?
content_blocks.accordion_body: Even with hearing aids, some places are still hard to hear in. ALDs give you extra clarity in tough situations, helping you stay confident and connected.

content_blocks.accordion_title: Can ALDs replace hearing aids?
content_blocks.accordion_body: No. ALDs are helpful tools but not a full replacement for hearing aids if you have moderate to severe hearing loss. Most people get the best results using both together.

content_blocks.heading_text: Assistive Listening Devices in Michigan, Minnesota & Florida

content_blocks.body_content:
At America’s Best Hearing, we believe everyone deserves to hear life more clearly. Our audiologists and hearing instrument specialists can help you choose the right assistive listening device for your needs.

Call or visit one of our Michigan, Minnesota, or Florida locations today to learn more about assistive listening devices like CapTel, CaptionCall, TV streamers, and more.

data.title: oticon FAQ 1
data.question: How long do Oticon hearing aids last?

children.text: Most in-the-ear models last around 5 years, while behind-the-ear models last 5–6 years. With good care, they may last longer, but many people upgrade sooner for newer features.

data.title: oticon FAQ 2
data.question: What is Oticon’s newest hearing aid?

children.text: The newest model, Oticon Intent, was released in 2024. It uses sensors to adjust to your activity and offers advanced 360-degree awareness.

data.title: oticon FAQ 3
data.question: Which Oticon hearing aids support tinnitus relief?

children.text: Models like Oticon Intent, Real, Own, Zircon, CROS, Xceed, and Play PX include Tinnitus SoundSupport™ for soothing relief sounds.

data.question: What are the levels of Phonak Lumity?

. L90 has the most advanced features, while L30 covers the basics.

data.title: phonak FAQ 2
data.question: What is Phonak Life?

children.text: Phonak Audéo Life™ Lumity is the first waterproof rechargeable hearing aid. It is perfect for people who are inclined to sports or water activities, or who live an active lifestyle.

data.title: phonak FAQ 3
data.question: How is Phonak Life different from Phonak Paradise?

children.text: Both share advanced features, but Phonak Life offers stronger water resistance, making it better for exercise, swimming, or humid environments.

data.title: resound FAQ 1
data.question: What are the levels of ReSound technology?

children.text: The newest Omnia hearing aids come in three levels: 9 (premium), 7 (mid-level), and 5 (basic).

data.title: resound FAQ 2
data.question: How do I adjust my ReSound hearing aids?

children.text: You can use the ReSound Smart 3D app, available for iPhone® and Android™. It allows you to adjust sound, manage tinnitus, and even get remote support from your audiologist.

data.title: resound FAQ 3
data.question: How long do ReSound hearing aids last?

children.text: Most last about 5 years, depending on care. Many people upgrade earlier to take advantage of new features and technology.

data.title: signia FAQ 1
data.question: What is the newest Signia technology?

children.text: The AX platform is Signia’s latest. Models like Styletto AX, Pure AX, and Insio Charge&Go AX use Augmented Focus technology to make speech clearer, even in noisy environments.

data.title: signia FAQ 2
data.question: What technology levels are available for Pure Charge&Go AX?

children.text: There are five levels: 1AX, 2AX, 3AX, 5AX, and 7AX. Higher numbers mean more advanced features.

data.title: signia FAQ 3
data.question: Can I adjust my Signia hearing aids myself?

children.text: Yes. With the Signia app, you can adjust volume, switch listening programs, or even change sound tone (bass/treble) from your phone.

data.title: starkey FAQ 1
data.question: What technology levels are available in Genesis AI?

children.text: There are four levels: 24 (highest), 20, 16, and 12 (entry level). A CROS model is also available for people with one-sided hearing loss.

data.title: starkey FAQ 2
data.question: What app works with Starkey hearing aids?

children.text: The My Starkey app connects with Genesis AI. It lets you adjust settings, track wellness, stream calls, and locate lost devices.

data.title: starkey FAQ 3
data.question: How long do Starkey hearing aids last?

children.text: In-the-ear styles usually last about 5 years, while behind-the-ear models last 5–6 years. Proper care can extend their life, but many people choose to upgrade sooner to enjoy the latest technology.

data.title: unitron FAQ 1
data.question: What is the difference between Unitron Blu and Vivante?

children.text: Vivante (V) is the newest generation of Unitron RIC hearing aids, offering improved sound processing and app support. Blu (B) remains the current option for custom in-the-ear devices.

data.title: unitron FAQ 2
data.question: What technology levels are available?

children.text: Unitron hearing aids come in four levels: 3, 5, 7, and 9. Higher levels (like 9) offer stronger noise reduction and more automatic adjustments.

data.title: unitron FAQ 3
data.question: How long does it take to charge Unitron hearing aids?

children.text: Charging takes about 5–7 hours. A green light indicates when the devices are fully charged.

children.text: The Widex Moment Sheer 440 is considered the premium model, with advanced features like speech enhancement and wind noise reduction.

data.title: widex FAQ 2

data.title: widex FAQ 3
data.question: Is Widex Moment good for tinnitus?

children.text: Yes! Widex Moment hearing aids come with Zen programs that play soothing sounds designed to make tinnitus less noticeable and easier to manage.
